title: Reading and Use of English – Part 2
description: For questions 9–16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only ONE word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: In the field of psychology, the concept of 'flow' describes a state of total immersion in (0) an activity, where time seems to vanish. (9) Since being identified by Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, this mental state is characterized by complete concentration and enjoyment. (10) Whether you are painting a masterpiece or solving a complex puzzle, being in flow means your skills are perfectly balanced (11) against the challenge at hand. If the task is too easy, boredom sets (12) in; if it is too difficult, anxiety arises. (13) Not until this equilibrium is reached can an individual experience the sense of effortless control that defines the flow state. Research suggests that flow (14) not only improves performance but also contributes significantly to overall well-being. (15) Despite its benefits, achieving flow requires a clear goal and immediate feedback. (16) Such is the power of this phenomenon that it can transform mundane tasks into deeply rewarding experiences. Many people report feeling a profound sense of satisfaction after a period of intense focus. Understanding how to cultivate this state can help us lead more fulfilling lives, as we learn to engage more deeply with our passions, ensuring that every moment spent is truly meaningful today.

example.text: In the field of psychology, the concept of 'flow' describes a state of total immersion in (0) an activity, where time seems to vanish.
example.explanation: Indefinite article required before a noun starting with a vowel sound.

questions.explanation: Conjunction/Preposition indicating the time from which something started.
Conjunction used to introduce two or more possibilities.
Preposition used here to show a comparison or balance between two forces.
Part of the phrasal verb 'set in', meaning to begin and likely continue.
Part of the 'Not until' structure which triggers negative inversion.
Part of the 'not only... but also' structure.
Preposition used to introduce a contrasting idea or concession.
Determiner used for emphasis at the beginning of a sentence, followed by an inversion.
